--- tags: [study-cluster, Computer-Vision, cluster-10] cluster: 10 --- # Cluster 10 — Cheat sheet (ICP) ## Definitions | Term | One-liner | |---|---| | ICP | Iteratively align two point clouds with unknown correspondences | | Source $X$ | Cloud being moved | | Target $Y$ | Reference cloud | | Rigid transform | $(R, t)$ — rotation + translation, no scaling | | Kabsch | Closed-form rigid alignment when correspondences are known | | RANSAC | Random-sample consensus — global initial alignment | ## Algorithm ``` init transform (R, t) ← initial guess loop: 1. for each x_i in X: find nearest y_j in Y # tentative correspondences 2. (R, t) ← kabsch( {(x_i, y_{j(i)})} ) # closed-form fit 3. X ← R @ X + t # apply 4. if change in transform / SSE small: break ``` ## Decision tree — alignment | Situation | Use | |---|---| | **Correspondences known** | **Kabsch** (single SVD, no iteration) | | **Correspondences unknown, clouds roughly aligned** (Q9) | **ICP** | | Clouds totally unaligned | RANSAC / feature matching → ICP refinement | | Need outlier tolerance | Trimmed / Robust ICP | | Smooth surfaces, fast convergence | Point-to-plane ICP | ## Kabsch (the inner step) ``` 1. ẋ = x − x̄ (centre source) 2. ẏ = y − ȳ (centre target) 3. H = Σ ẋ_i ẏ_iᵀ (covariance) 4. SVD: H = U Σ Vᵀ 5. R = V Uᵀ 6. t = ȳ − R x̄ ``` ## Properties | Property | Value | |---|---| | Optimisation | Non-convex | | Convergence | Monotonic in SSE per iteration | | Local minima | Many — needs good init | | Per-iteration cost | $O(N \log N)$ NN search + $O(1)$ SVD on $3\times 3$ | | Outlier robustness | Low (vanilla); high (trimmed / robust variants) | | Scale | Rigid only; needs separate handling for similarity / affine | ## Common traps - **ICP needs a good initial alignment.** Don't expect it to recover from a 90° rotation off — use RANSAC (or feature matching) to get a rough init first. - **ICP doesn't handle scale.** If the two clouds are at different scales, vanilla ICP fails — use a similarity-transform variant. - **Don't run ICP when correspondences are known** — that's just Kabsch, no iteration needed. - **Nearest-neighbour ≠ correct correspondence.** ICP's NN step *guesses*; in early iterations these guesses are often wrong, but the loop refines them. - **Sensitive to outliers** in vanilla form — a few bad NN pairs can pull the whole alignment off. Use Trimmed or Robust ICP for noisy data. - **Point-to-point vs point-to-plane.** Point-to-plane converges faster on smooth surfaces but needs surface normals. - **Q9 trap distractors.** ICP isn't for *finding* correspondences from features (that's matching), isn't a *descriptor* (that's SIFT/HoG), and isn't a *clustering* algorithm. ## Pipeline (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR init(["X (source)
Y (target)
initial guess"]) --> nn["NN(X, Y)
tentative pairs"] nn --> kab["Kabsch on pairs
→ R, t"] kab --> apply["X ← R·X + t"] apply --> conv{"converged?"} conv -->|"no"| nn conv -->|"yes"| done(["aligned X"]) classDef step fill:#e3f2fd classDef out fill:#fff3e0 class nn,kab,apply step class done out ```

# Cluster 11 — Shape descriptors (binary objects)

**Exam coverage:** Q22 (0.3) — **0.30 pts total**, single application question. Blueprint flags this as a "skip candidate" (0.3 pts at difficulty 3) — don't over-invest, but the recall is concrete enough to lock in.

## Lectures involved

- [[../../../../10-Courses/Computer-Vision/lectures/lecture-13-binary-object-characterization]]

## Concept notes

- [[../../../../20-Concepts/shape-descriptors]] — area, perimeter, convex hull, compactness, **solidity**
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/image-moments]] — centroid, orientation, eccentricity
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/connected-components]] — prerequisite labelling
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/binary-image]] — input

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../lecture-13]]

## Source slides

[Slides ↗](../../../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/13_binary_object_characterization(1).pdf)

## What this cluster is about

After thresholding an image into a binary mask and splitting it into connected components, we measure each object with cheap, interpretable **shape descriptors**. These were the workhorses of pre-deep-learning industrial vision (sorting parts on a conveyor, counting grains, characterising biological structures) and still appear as features in classical pipelines.

The ones to know:
- **Area** = count of foreground pixels.
- **Perimeter** = boundary length (grid-count or marching-squares approximation).
- **Convex hull** = smallest convex polygon containing the object.
- **Compactness / circularity** = $4\pi A / P^2$ — equal to 1 for a perfect circle, falling toward 0 for elongated or irregular shapes.
- **Solidity** = $A / A_\text{hull}$ — equal to 1 for any convex shape, falling below 1 when the object has **concavities or holes**.

**Q22 asks for solidity interpretation**: a low solidity tells you the object's area is much smaller than its convex hull's area, which means there are **substantial concavities** carved out of the convex envelope — branches, cavities, articulated limbs. A horse silhouette has many concavities (under the belly, between legs); a dumbbell shape has a deep concavity in the middle; a starburst or branching structure has very low solidity. By contrast, blocky/convex objects (rectangles, circles, triangles) all have solidity near 1. Don't confuse solidity with **compactness** (which measures "roundness" via perimeter) or **eccentricity** (which measures elongation via second-moment eigenvalues). They're distinct: a long thin rectangle is convex (solidity ≈ 1) but elongated (eccentricity high) and not compact (compactness low).

content.newText:
---
tags: [study-cluster, Computer-Vision, cluster-11]
cluster: 11
---

# Cluster 11 — Atoms (Shape descriptors)

## Pipeline (context)

1. **Prerequisites.** Threshold (e.g. Otsu, or a DL mask) → binary image → **connected components** labelling → per-object measurements [[connected-components]] — [slide 4](../../../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/13_binary_object_characterization(1).pdf#page=4).
2. **4- vs 8-connectivity.** 4-connectivity considers only N/S/E/W neighbours; 8-connectivity adds diagonals. Choice changes which pixel groups count as one component.

## The five shape descriptors

3. **Area.** Number of foreground pixels in the connected component [[shape-descriptors]].
4. **Perimeter.** Boundary length. Two approximations: grid-count (count missing 4-neighbours per foreground pixel — fast, biased on rotated shapes) or marching-squares contour length (more accurate).
5. **Convex hull.** Smallest convex polygon containing all foreground pixels. `cv2.convexHull` / `skimage.morphology.convex_hull_image`. Used as the reference shape for **solidity**.
6. **Compactness / circularity.** $f_\text{circ} = \dfrac{4\pi\,\text{Area}}{\text{Perimeter}^2}$ — [slide 12](../../../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/13_binary_object_characterization(1).pdf#page=12).
   - = **1** for a perfect circle.
   - Falls toward 0 for elongated or boundary-irregular shapes.
7. **Solidity.** $\text{solidity} = \dfrac{\text{Area}}{\text{Area of convex hull}}$.
   - = **1** for any convex shape (rectangle, circle, triangle).
   - **< 1** when the object has concavities or holes — convex hull is bigger than the actual area.

## Q22 — solidity interpretation

8. **Q22 answer.** Low solidity ⇒ the convex hull is much larger than the actual area ⇒ the object has **substantial concavities** (branches, articulated limbs, holes, deep notches). A horse silhouette, a starburst, a dumbbell, a branching tree.
9. **High solidity (≈ 1)** ⇒ object roughly fills its convex envelope ⇒ **convex or near-convex** (rectangle, circle, triangle, ellipse).

## Don't confuse with

10. **Compactness / circularity** measures **roundness** via boundary length. A long thin rectangle has solidity ≈ 1 (convex) but compactness very low (perimeter² huge vs area).
11. **Eccentricity** $e = \sqrt{1 - \lambda_2/\lambda_1}$ measures **elongation** via second-moment eigenvalues. Convex but stretched ⇒ solidity ≈ 1, eccentricity ≈ 1 [[image-moments]].
12. **Three independent properties.**
    - **Solidity**: convex vs concave.
    - **Compactness**: round vs irregular.
    - **Eccentricity**: round vs elongated.

## Image moments (helpful adjacent context)

13. **Centroid (first moment).** $(\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = \frac{1}{N}(\sum x_i, \sum y_i)$ — [slide 13](../../../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/13_binary_object_characterization(1).pdf#page=13).
14. **Second-moment matrix.** $M = \begin{pmatrix} \mu_{20} & \mu_{11} \\ \mu_{11} & \mu_{02} \end{pmatrix}$ with $\mu_{20} = \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$, $\mu_{02} = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2$, $\mu_{11} = \sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$.
15. **Orientation.** $\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{atan2}(2\mu_{11}, \mu_{20} - \mu_{02})$ — angle of the major axis.
16. **Eccentricity.** $e = \sqrt{1 - \lambda_2/\lambda_1}$ where $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2$ are eigenvalues of $M$. 0 for a circle, 1 for a line.

--- tags: [study-cluster, Computer-Vision, cluster-11] cluster: 11 --- # Cluster 11 — Cheat sheet (Shape descriptors) ## Definitions | Term | Formula / value | |---|---| | Area $A$ | Count of foreground pixels | | Perimeter $P$ | Boundary length (grid-count or contour) | | Convex hull | Smallest convex polygon containing the object | | **Compactness** $f_\text{circ}$ | $4\pi A / P^2$ — measures **roundness** | | **Solidity** | $A / A_\text{hull}$ — measures **convexity** | | **Eccentricity** $e$ | $\sqrt{1 - \lambda_2/\lambda_1}$ — measures **elongation** | | Centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ | First moment, mean of foreground coords | | Orientation $\theta$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{atan2}(2\mu_{11}, \mu_{20} - \mu_{02})$ | ## Three independent properties | Descriptor | What it measures | "1" means | "near 0" means | |---|---|---|---| | **Compactness** | Roundness | Perfect circle | Highly elongated / boundary-irregular | | **Solidity** | Convexity | Convex shape | Many concavities / holes / branches | | **Eccentricity** | Elongation | Line-like | Circular | ## Q22 — solidity interpretation table | Shape | Solidity | Why | |---|---|---| | Circle / ellipse | 1.0 | convex | | Rectangle / square / triangle | 1.0 | convex | | Long thin bar | ≈ 1.0 | convex (thin but no concavities) | | Letter "L" | ≈ 0.5 | concavity in inside corner | | Letter "U" | ≈ 0.6 | open mouth = concavity | | Letter "X" | ≈ 0.5 | four concavities between arms | | Star / starburst | ≈ 0.4 | many concavities | | Horse silhouette | low | belly, between legs | | Dumbbell shape | very low | deep central concavity | **Rule:** low solidity ⇒ many concavities, branches, or holes. ## Don't confuse | Pair | How they differ | |---|---| | Solidity vs compactness | Solidity = convex envelope; compactness = roundness via perimeter | | Solidity vs eccentricity | Solidity = convex/concave; eccentricity = round/elongated | | Compactness vs eccentricity | Compactness uses perimeter; eccentricity uses second-moment matrix | | Long thin bar | high solidity, low compactness, high eccentricity | | Horse silhouette | low solidity, low compactness, moderate eccentricity | | Donut (with hole) | low solidity, low compactness, low eccentricity | ## Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR img(["grayscale image"]) --> thr["threshold
(Otsu / DL mask)"] thr --> bin(["binary image"]) bin --> cc["connected components
4- or 8-connectivity"] cc --> labelled(["labelled regions"]) labelled --> A["area"] labelled --> P["perimeter"] labelled --> H["convex hull → solidity"] labelled --> M["moments → centroid, orientation, eccentricity"] classDef stage fill:#e3f2fd classDef metric fill:#fff3e0 class thr,cc stage class A,P,H,M metric ``` ## Common traps - **Solidity ≠ compactness ≠ eccentricity.** Three different shape properties with three different formulas. - **A convex elongated bar has solidity ≈ 1 but compactness very low.** Don't conflate. - **Solidity counts concavities, not size.** A small concave object can have lower solidity than a large convex one. - **Holes count toward solidity loss.** A donut has lower solidity than a disc of the same outer radius (the central hole reduces area but not hull area). - **Perimeter approximation matters.** Grid-count overestimates on rotated shapes; marching-squares is more accurate. Affects compactness. - **8-connectivity vs 4-connectivity** can change the connected-components count (and therefore which pixels belong to which object). - **Solidity and compactness are related but not redundant.** A thin rectangle is convex (solidity 1) but elongated and so not compact. - **Q22 distractors.** Low solidity doesn't mean: small object, elongated, irregular boundary, or low contrast — it means **concave/branched**.

--- tags: [study-cluster, Computer-Vision, cluster-11] cluster: 11 --- # Cluster 11 — Cheat sheet (Shape descriptors) ## Definitions | Term | Formula / value | |---|---| | Area $A$ | Count of foreground pixels | | Perimeter $P$ | Boundary length (grid-count or contour) | | Convex hull | Smallest convex polygon containing the object | | **Compactness** $f_\text{circ}$ | $4\pi A / P^2$ — measures **roundness** | | **Solidity** | $A / A_\text{hull}$ — measures **convexity** | | **Eccentricity** $e$ | $\sqrt{1 - \lambda_2/\lambda_1}$ — measures **elongation** | | Centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ | First moment, mean of foreground coords | | Orientation $\theta$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{atan2}(2\mu_{11}, \mu_{20} - \mu_{02})$ | ## Three independent properties | Descriptor | What it measures | "1" means | "near 0" means | |---|---|---|---| | **Compactness** | Roundness | Perfect circle | Highly elongated / boundary-irregular | | **Solidity** | Convexity | Convex shape | Many concavities / holes / branches | | **Eccentricity** | Elongation | Line-like | Circular | ## Q22 — solidity interpretation table | Shape | Solidity | Why | |---|---|---| | Circle / ellipse | 1.0 | convex | | Rectangle / square / triangle | 1.0 | convex | | Long thin bar | ≈ 1.0 | convex (thin but no concavities) | | Letter "L" | ≈ 0.5 | concavity in inside corner | | Letter "U" | ≈ 0.6 | open mouth = concavity | | Letter "X" | ≈ 0.5 | four concavities between arms | | Star / starburst | ≈ 0.4 | many concavities | | Horse silhouette | low | belly, between legs | | Dumbbell shape | very low | deep central concavity | **Rule:** low solidity ⇒ many concavities, branches, or holes. ## Don't confuse | Pair | How they differ | |---|---| | Solidity vs compactness | Solidity = convex envelope; compactness = roundness via perimeter | | Solidity vs eccentricity | Solidity = convex/concave; eccentricity = round/elongated | | Compactness vs eccentricity | Compactness uses perimeter; eccentricity uses second-moment matrix | | Long thin bar | high solidity, low compactness, high eccentricity | | Horse silhouette | low solidity, low compactness, moderate eccentricity | | Donut (with hole) | low solidity, low compactness, low eccentricity | ## Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR img(["grayscale image"]) --> thr["threshold
(Otsu / DL mask)"] thr --> bin(["binary image"]) bin --> cc["connected components
4- or 8-connectivity"] cc --> labelled(["labelled regions"]) labelled --> A["area"] labelled --> P["perimeter"] labelled --> H["convex hull → solidity"] labelled --> M["moments → centroid, orientation, eccentricity"] classDef stage fill:#e3f2fd classDef metric fill:#fff3e0 class thr,cc stage class A,P,H,M metric ``` ## Common traps - **Solidity ≠ compactness ≠ eccentricity.** Three different shape properties with three different formulas. - **A convex elongated bar has solidity ≈ 1 but compactness very low.** Don't conflate. - **Solidity counts concavities, not size.** A small concave object can have lower solidity than a large convex one. - **Holes count toward solidity loss.** A donut has lower solidity than a disc of the same outer radius (the central hole reduces area but not hull area). - **Perimeter approximation matters.** Grid-count overestimates on rotated shapes; marching-squares is more accurate. Affects compactness. - **8-connectivity vs 4-connectivity** can change the connected-components count (and therefore which pixels belong to which object). - **Solidity and compactness are related but not redundant.** A thin rectangle is convex (solidity 1) but elongated and so not compact. - **Q22 distractors.** Low solidity doesn't mean: small object, elongated, irregular boundary, or low contrast — it means **concave/branched**.

# Cluster 12 — Self-supervised & CLIP

**Exam coverage:** Q12 (0.3) — **0.30 pts total**, single-fact recall.

## Lectures involved

- [[../../../../10-Courses/Computer-Vision/lectures/lecture-19-self-supervised-and-contrastive]]

## Concept notes

- [[../../../../20-Concepts/clip]] — Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/contrastive-learning]] — pull positives close, push negatives apart
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/self-supervised-learning]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/zero-shot-learning]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/triplet-loss]]
- [[../../../../20-Concepts/simclr]]

## Per-lecture flashcards

- [[../../lecture-19]]

## Source slides

[Slides ↗](../../../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/19_self_supervised_and_contrastive_learning.pdf)

## What this cluster is about

**CLIP** (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining, OpenAI 2021) is a multimodal foundation model trained contrastively on **400 million (image, caption) pairs** scraped from the web. Two encoders — one for images (ResNet or ViT), one for text (transformer) — both project into a shared $d$-dimensional embedding space, where matched (image, caption) pairs are close in cosine similarity and unmatched pairs are far apart.

**Q12's training objective** is exactly this contrastive image-text alignment: for a batch of $N$ image-text pairs, build the $N \times N$ cosine-similarity matrix between image embeddings $I_i$ and text embeddings $T_j$. The diagonal entries are **positives** (image $i$ should match its own caption); the off-diagonals are **negatives** (image $i$ should not match anyone else's caption). Apply NT-Xent / softmax cross-entropy in **both directions** — image-to-text and text-to-text — and that's the loss. There's no per-image label, no class to predict; the supervision is purely "this image goes with this caption, not those other captions in the batch".

The payoff is **zero-shot classification**: any new image-classification task can be solved without further training by phrasing class names as text prompts ("A photo of a cat", "A photo of a dog", …), encoding all prompts via the text encoder, and picking the class with the highest cosine similarity to the image embedding. Empirically, CLIP zero-shot reaches ~76% top-1 on ImageNet — matching a fully-supervised ResNet-50 trained directly on ImageNet labels — and **dramatically beats** ResNet on distribution shifts (sketches, paintings, unusual poses, adversarial examples). The contrastive image-text objective forces the encoder to learn what makes a banana a banana **across many depictions**, not just photographic ones.

content.newText:
---
tags: [study-cluster, Computer-Vision, cluster-12]
cluster: 12
---

# Cluster 12 — Atoms (Self-supervised & CLIP)

## CLIP training (Q12)

1. **Q12 answer.** CLIP is trained to **maximise cosine similarity between matched (image, caption) pairs and minimise it for unmatched pairs** — i.e. align an image embedding with the embedding of its caption [[clip]] — [slide 35](../../../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/19_self_supervised_and_contrastive_learning.pdf#page=35).
2. **Two encoders, one shared space.** Image encoder (ResNet or ViT) + text encoder (transformer). A linear projection on each places both into the same $d$-dimensional embedding space; $\ell_2$-normalisation makes cosine similarity equal to dot product.
3. **Batch contrastive setup.** For batch of $N$ image-text pairs, compute the $N \times N$ similarity matrix $S_{ij} = I_i \cdot T_j$. **Diagonal = positives**, off-diagonal = negatives.
4. **Loss.** **NT-Xent / softmax cross-entropy** applied in **both directions** (image → text and text → image). The model is trained so that row $i$'s softmax-argmax is column $i$, and column $i$'s softmax-argmax is row $i$.
5. **No labels.** No class supervision. The supervision is purely "this caption goes with this image, not those other captions in the batch".
6. **Training data.** WIT (Wikipedia-based Image Text): **400M image-caption pairs** scraped from the web — two orders of magnitude more than ImageNet, lower quality but vastly broader semantic coverage.

## Zero-shot classification

7. **How CLIP enables zero-shot.** Encode the class names as text prompts ("A photo of a cat", "A photo of a dog", …) via the text encoder. Encode the new image via the image encoder. Pick the class with **highest cosine similarity** to the image embedding [[zero-shot-learning]].
8. **No fine-tuning, no extra data.** Just text prompts.
9. **Performance.** Zero-shot CLIP ≈ 76% top-1 on ImageNet — matches a fully-supervised ResNet-50 trained directly on ImageNet labels.

## Why CLIP generalises better

10. **Distribution-shift robustness.** On ImageNet itself, CLIP and ResNet-50 score the same. On distribution-shifted variants (paintings, sketches, unusual poses, adversarial), CLIP **dramatically beats** ResNet — e.g. ImageNet Rendition: ResNet-50 38% vs CLIP-ViT-L 89%; ImageNet Adversarial: 3% vs 77%.
11. **Why.** Contrastive image-text training forces the encoder to learn what makes a banana a banana **across many depictions** — photographs, drawings, sketches, in unusual contexts. ImageNet supervised training only sees photos.

## Contrastive learning (parent concept)

12. **Idea.** Shape the embedding space so similar inputs are close and dissimilar inputs are far apart [[contrastive-learning]].
13. **Two regimes.** Supervised contrastive (positives = same label) vs self-supervised contrastive (positives = augmentations of the same image, e.g. SimCLR).
14. **Loss family.**
    - **Margin / contrastive loss** — single positive or negative pair.
    - **Triplet loss** — (anchor, positive, negative): $d(a, p) < d(a, n) + M$.
    - **NT-Xent / InfoNCE** — one positive + many in-batch negatives, softmax cross-entropy. **Used by CLIP and SimCLR.**
15. **Collapse problem.** Without negatives, the trivial solution "embed everything as zero" minimises the loss. Three fixes: add negatives (margin/triplet/NT-Xent), asymmetric stop-gradient (BYOL/SimSiam), centring + sharpening (DINO).

## Self-supervised landscape (extra context)

16. **SimCLR.** Self-supervised contrastive on images: two augmented views of the same image are positives; other batch members are negatives. NT-Xent loss [[simclr]].
17. **BYOL / SimSiam.** No negatives — asymmetric predictor + stop-gradient on target network.
18. **DINO.** Student-teacher with centring and sharpening; produces strong semantic features.
19. **CLIP fits in this picture as multimodal SSL** — positives are (image, caption) instead of two augmentations of the same image.

## Limitations of CLIP

20. **Weak on specialised domains.** Medical scans, satellite imagery, MNIST-style stylised data — not in WIT, so weak features.
21. **Weak on fine-grained spatial reasoning** (counting, precise positions).
22. **Weak when classes can't be described in natural language** — the text encoder is the bottleneck.

content.newText:
--- tags: [study-cluster, Computer-Vision, cluster-12] cluster: 12 --- # Cluster 12 — Cheat sheet (Self-supervised & CLIP) ## Definitions | Term | One-liner | |---|---| | CLIP | Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (OpenAI 2021) | | Image encoder | ResNet or ViT, projected to shared embedding space | | Text encoder | Transformer, projected to same shared space | | Shared embedding space | $d$-dim, $\ell_2$-normalised → cosine = dot product | | WIT | Wikipedia-based Image Text — 400M (image, caption) pairs | | NT-Xent / InfoNCE | Softmax cross-entropy with positives + in-batch negatives | | Zero-shot classification | Classify by text prompts, no further training | | Contrastive learning | Pull positives close, push negatives apart | ## Key formulas | Quantity | Formula | |---|---| | Similarity matrix | $S_{ij} = I_i \cdot T_j$ ($N \times N$ for batch of $N$) | | Loss (image → text) | $\mathcal{L}_{i\to t} = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_i \log \dfrac{\exp(S_{ii}/\tau)}{\sum_j \exp(S_{ij}/\tau)}$ | | Loss (text → image) | $\mathcal{L}_{t\to i} = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_i \log \dfrac{\exp(S_{ii}/\tau)}{\sum_j \exp(S_{ji}/\tau)}$ | | Total | $\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathcal{L}_{i\to t} + \mathcal{L}_{t\to i})$ | | Zero-shot prediction | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \cos(I, T_c)$ | ## Q12 — what's the training objective **Answer:** CLIP is trained with a **contrastive image-text alignment** objective: matched (image, caption) pairs are pulled close in cosine similarity; unmatched pairs are pushed apart. **Mechanics:** 1. Encode batch of $N$ images and $N$ captions. 2. Build $N \times N$ cosine-similarity matrix $S$. 3. Diagonal entries are positives; off-diagonals are negatives. 4. Apply softmax cross-entropy in both directions. ## CLIP training (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR img(["N images I_1..I_N"]) --> ie["Image encoder
(ViT or ResNet)"] txt(["N captions T_1..T_N"]) --> te["Text encoder
(transformer)"] ie --> iemb["I embeddings
(N × d)"] te --> temb["T embeddings
(N × d)"] iemb --> sim["S = I @ Tᵀ
(N × N cosine sim)"] temb --> sim sim --> ntx["NT-Xent both directions
diagonal = positives"] classDef enc fill:#e3f2fd classDef loss fill:#fff3e0 class ie,te enc class sim,ntx loss ``` ## Zero-shot classification (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR classes(["class names
cat, dog, ..."]) --> prompt["wrap as
'A photo of a {class}'"] prompt --> tenc["text encoder"] tenc --> tembs["T_1..T_K"] img(["new image"]) --> ienc["image encoder"] ienc --> iemb["I"] iemb --> sim["cosine sim
I · T_k"] tembs --> sim sim --> argmax["argmax → class"] ``` ## Distribution-shift robustness (intuition table) | Dataset | ResNet-50 (supervised) | CLIP ViT-L | |---|---|---| | ImageNet | 76% | 76% | | ImageNet V2 | 64% | 70% | | ImageNet Rendition | 38% | **89%** | | ObjectNet | 33% | **72%** | | ImageNet Sketch | 25% | **60%** | | ImageNet Adversarial | 3% | **77%** | ## When-to-use | Goal | Use | |---|---| | Zero-shot classification | CLIP via text prompts | | Robust to distribution shift | CLIP backbone | | Image-text retrieval | CLIP cosine similarity | | Fine-grained / specialised domain | NOT CLIP — fine-tune a domain model | | Self-supervised image features (no text) | SimCLR / DINO / MoCo | | Few labels available | Linear probe on CLIP embeddings | ## Common traps - **CLIP isn't trained with labels.** No "cat", "dog" labels — only "matches caption / doesn't match caption". - **Both directions of the loss.** Image→text and text→image. Skipping one degrades performance. - **Positives are exactly the diagonal of the batch similarity matrix** — not "augmentations of the same image" (that's SimCLR). - **Zero-shot ≠ no model.** It's no *fine-tuning*; the pre-trained CLIP weights do all the work. - **CLIP is multimodal SSL** — same family as SimCLR but with text-image pairs as positives instead of augmentation pairs. - **Contrastive losses can collapse to a constant** without negatives. CLIP's in-batch negatives prevent this. - **Q12 distractor: "image classification with cross-entropy"** — that's supervised ImageNet training, not CLIP. - **Q12 distractor: "predict the caption from the image"** — that's image captioning (a generative task), not CLIP. CLIP only matches; it doesn't generate.

# Computer Vision — Cluster index

Twelve cluster artifacts derived from `40-Reviews/Computer-Vision/exam-blueprint.md`. Each cluster has three files:

- **overview** — orientation, lectures + concepts involved, conceptual through-line
- **atoms** — atomic facts for memorisation, with slide-page links
- **cheat-sheet** — one-page exam-day reference (tables, formulas, traps)

## Master table

| #  | Cluster                          | Marks | Questions               | Lectures | Overview                                              | Atoms                                              | Cheat sheet                                              |
| -- | -------------------------------- | ----- | ----------------------- | -------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| 01 | Fourier & sampling               | 1.95  | Q2, Q7, Q11, Q16, Q25   | 10       | [[01-fourier-and-sampling/overview\|→]]               | [[01-fourier-and-sampling/atoms\|→]]               | [[01-fourier-and-sampling/cheat-sheet\|→]]               |
